name: Webiny Open-Source Serverless CMS API
what_it_does: Open-source serverless headless CMS that deploys to your AWS account with GraphQL API — enabling teams to run a fully managed CMS on their own AWS infrastructure using Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, and CloudFront, with content modeling, multi-tenancy, role-based access, and page builder, without vendor lock-in or SaaS fees.
use_cases: Delivering headless content via Webiny GraphQL API for Next.js or Gatsby frontends on own AWS
Managing Webiny content models and entries via GraphQL API for content migration automation
Building multi-tenant CMS applications via Webiny API for SaaS product teams
Customizing Webiny CMS with plugins and extensions via API for enterprise content workflows
Managing Webiny page builder content via API for landing page automation
not_for: Teams without AWS expertise (Webiny deploys to your AWS — requires AWS knowledge)
Simple projects where deploying a serverless CMS to AWS is overkill
Teams needing a fully managed SaaS CMS without infrastructure management
best_when: An enterprise or privacy-focused team wants a GraphQL headless CMS running entirely on their own AWS account — with no vendor lock-in, multi-tenancy, and serverless scale without SaaS fees.
avoid_when: You don't have AWS expertise, need SaaS simplicity, or have a small project where AWS deployment overhead isn't justified.

auth.notes: GraphQL-only API (no REST). Bearer token auth for API requests. Cognito or custom Auth for user authentication (configurable). API keys for headless delivery (public read access). Webhook for content events. TypeScript/JavaScript SDK. Multi-tenant auth with tenant isolation. Role-based access control (RBAC). Security scopes per content model. AWS IAM for infrastructure access.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Open source (MIT) self-hosted on AWS; only AWS infrastructure costs
pricing.notes: London company. Founded 2019 by Sven Al Hamad and Adrian Smijulj. Raised $3.5M seed. Open source MIT license. GitHub: webiny/webiny-js (8,000+ stars). Deploys to user's AWS account via Pulumi IaC. Uses: Lambda (compute), DynamoDB (data), S3 (files), CloudFront (CDN), ElasticSearch/OpenSearch (search). Serverless-first architecture — scales to zero cost. Page Builder included (no-code page creation). Form Builder included. Multi-tenancy built-in. Growing in enterprise and government use cases.

performance.rate_limits: AWS Lambda limits (1,000 concurrent by default)

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: GraphQL-only API (no REST). Bearer token auth. Webhook for content events. TypeScript SDK. Requires AWS deployment. Open source — can inspect GraphQL schema. Good documentation for AWS deployment. Rate limits governed by AWS Lambda concurrency. User controls all data and compliance.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: GraphQL queries fully idempotent. Content reads idempotent by ID. Mutations return deterministic results for same input. API keys provide consistent read access.
known_agent_gotchas: GraphQL only — no REST API; requires GraphQL client
Deploys to YOUR AWS account — AWS credentials and deployment setup required
Cold starts on Lambda — first request after idle may be slow (300-800ms extra)
Pulumi IaC for deployment — requires Pulumi knowledge for infrastructure changes
Multi-tenancy requires understanding of Webiny tenant concept for multi-site agents

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced. User-controlled data residency and compliance (deploys to own AWS). AWS IAM for infrastructure. Cognito or custom auth for users. API keys for read-only delivery (safe for client-side). Role-based access control per content model. Multi-tenant isolation at application level. Open source — auditable code. Compliance depends on user's AWS account configuration and certifications.
